title: Episode 20: A Casual Chat with Rodger Deering: Dungeon Crusade! Video Games! Fetch Hounds! And More!
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=I70TmekxAXE

A long-form Hobbycast conversation between Daniel (@dungeondive) and Roger Pearce (Groovus Games) — a rare full-window into the man behind Dungeon Crusade. Roger opens with the announcement of his new **video encyclopedia** series: two-to-three-minute focused videos on individual game systems (lock picking, patrol routes, partying heroes, special abilities), explicitly designed against his own reputation for verbose long-form coverage.

The middle of the chat wanders happily — Roger's love of horde and survival modes in GTA5 (he builds his own creator-tool wave maps), Call of Duty bot campaigns, his appreciation for what Rockstar would do with a fantasy world; Daniel's affection for single-screen Metroidvanias on a laptop that can't run anything modern. Then the origin story: Dungeon Crusade started in 2008, fourteen years of revision when this episode aired, sculpted from clay-of-influences by Dark Tower, Dungeon Quest, Warhammer Quest, HeroQuest, Baldur's Gate, Icewind Dale, Eye of the Beholder, and Ultima. Roger tells the story of his friend Chris Rothgery and the Lakewood, Ohio, game shop where he first laid eyes on Dungeon Quest in the 80s.

Roger turns the interview around at the end and asks Daniel about Mage Knight ("a math problem… too deterministic"), Legends of Andor (a puzzle dressed as an adventure, but a beautiful one), Gloomhaven and Jaws of the Lion (respectful but not for him), and where Daniel sits on randomness versus streamlined euros (firmly with the dice). Closes with the giveaway pitch.

----

Roger's pitch for the *video encyclopedia* format is short videos that index a complex game's systems one at a time. Would you actually use a series like that to *learn* a game, or only as reference once you already half-know it?
